parasitic_pathogens: Intracellular protozoan that infects intestinal epithelial cells and causes watery diarrhea and mucosal inflammation.
Invasive protozoan that degrades intestinal tissue and can cause dysentery and mucosal ulceration.
Flagellated protozoan that attaches to the small intestine, disrupting nutrient absorption and causing diarrhea and dysbiosis.
viral_pathogens: Enteric virus that infects intestinal epithelial cells and is associated with pediatric gastroenteritis and diarrhea.
fungi: Opportunistic yeast that can expand during microbiome imbalance and contribute to mucosal irritation and immune activation.
Opportunistic fungal pathobiont capable of epithelial adhesion and biofilm formation, promoting inflammation when overgrown.
protozoa_worms: Intestinal protozoan that colonizes the colon; may alter gut permeability and trigger inflammatory responses in some hosts.
Flagellated protozoan commonly found as a commensal; elevated levels may reflect microbial imbalance or gastrointestinal irritation.
Hookworm that attaches to intestinal mucosa and feeds on blood, potentially causing anemia and intestinal inflammation.
Large intestinal roundworm that competes for nutrients and may cause intestinal obstruction or malabsorption in heavy infections.
Hookworm that penetrates skin and attaches to intestinal mucosa, feeding on blood and potentially causing iron-deficiency anemia.
Whipworm that embeds in colonic mucosa, causing local inflammation, diarrhea, and potential anemia in heavy infections.
Intestinal tapeworm that absorbs host nutrients and may cause abdominal discomfort and nutritional deficiencies.
